steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: This is a Boundaries question testing how to punctuate an appositive. The phrase "the explosion of a massive star, which blasts large amounts of radiation, heavy elements, and debris into space" renames "supernova" and must be set off by matching punctuation on both sides — an opening mark before "the" and a closing mark after "space."

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — "supernova: the": A colon can introduce an appositive, but only a matching colon (or dash) can close it. The closing em dash after "space" doesn't pair with a colon, so the enclosure is mismatched. ✗
  • Choice B — "supernova, the": A comma opens the appositive but the sentence closes it with an em dash, creating mismatched punctuation around the phrase. ✗
  • Choice C — "supernova. The": A period ends the sentence after "supernova," making "The explosion of a massive star..." a new sentence that lacks its own verb and leaves the original clause without its noun phrase. Creates a fragment. ✗
  • Choice D — "supernova—the": An opening em dash before "the" pairs with the closing em dash after "space," correctly enclosing the appositive with matching punctuation on both sides. ✓
That matches choice D — D is correct.
